text: {"overallConclusion":"","inferences":[{"type":"evidence-for","premiseIndexes":[1],"conclusionIndex":0,"warrant":"Evidence from a subgroup (type‑2‑diabetes participants) that shows increased risk can be taken as supporting the broader claim that eggs raise cardiovascular risk","strength":"moderate","defeaters":["The effect may be limited to diabetics and not apply to the general population","Potential confounding variables specific to the diabetic subgroup","Other studies showing no association"]},{"type":"supports","premiseIndexes":[0],"conclusionIndex":-1,"warrant":"A direct empirical finding that eggs increase cardiovascular risk serves as a premise for the overall conclusion that eggs are a cardiovascular risk factor","strength":"moderate","defeaters":["Contradictory empirical evidence","Methodological flaws in the study reporting the risk"]},{"type":"evidence-for","premiseIndexes":[1],"conclusionIndex":-1,"warrant":"The observed association in diabetics provides additional empirical support for the overall claim that eggs increase cardiovascular risk","strength":"moderate","defeaters":["The association may be specific to diabetics and not generalizable","Uncontrolled confounders in the diabetic subgroup study"]}]}
